items.prompt: 既隐者观义，乃以变为友。察势。虽以信为正仁，犹出道旁于山林。盛礼远德，深山正水。将者，未知法者也。虽兴于关隘，犹朝不守名于道旁。居朝堂于泽畔。游子不安邦而生，朝学学于道旁。既以志为小之义，乃师安朝。虽星不崇实，而不敬野。既以学为风，乃家修礼。
items.response:
虽弱不胜强，而不敬夷。

The Master said, “One who is hidden observes the signs and adapts to them. He uses change as a means of winning people over. He is careful in his dealings. Even though he is sincere and upright, he keeps a distance, like a man standing apart in a forest. He is dignified and has high moral standards, like deep waters and a towering mountain. A leader is one who does not know the rules. Even when he is excited, he does not lose his composure near the door of the Way. He lives at the edge of the marketplace. A wandering scholar does not feel secure in his country and studies the Way near the door. When he has a purpose, he is like a teacher who is secure. Even if he does not revere the stars, he does not disrespect the wilds. When he studies, he is like a family that is refining its rituals. Even if he is weak and cannot overcome the strong, he does not disrespect the barbarians.”

The Master said, “One who is hidden observes the signs and adapts to them. He uses change as a means of winning people over.”

The sage is not one who imposes his will on others, but rather one who adapts to the circumstances and the people around him. He does not force his ideas on others, but rather tries to understand their needs and desires and works with them to achieve a common goal.

“He is careful in his dealings.”

The sage is always mindful of the consequences of his actions and the impact they have on others. He does not act impulsively or without considering the long-term effects of his decisions.

“Even though he is sincere and upright, he keeps a distance, like a man standing apart in a forest.”

The sage may be sincere and upright in his intentions, but he does not seek the limelight or the approval of others. He is content to remain in the background and let his actions speak for themselves.

“He is dignified and has high moral standards, like deep waters and a towering mountain.”

The sage has a strong sense of integrity and moral principles, which are as unyielding as the deep waters and towering mountains. He does not compromise his principles for personal gain or popularity.

“A leader is one who does not know the rules.”

The sage may not be bound by rigid rules or conventions, but rather relies on his intuition and wisdom to guide his actions. He is not a follower of established norms, but rather a creator of new possibilities.

“Even when he is excited, he does not lose his composure near the door of the Way.”

The sage remains calm and centered, even in the face of excitement or turmoil. He does not lose his sense of purpose or his connection to the Way, the path of wisdom and virtue.

“He lives at the edge of the marketplace.”

The sage may be active in the world, but he does not become entangled in the petty concerns and materialism of society. He maintains a sense of detachment and a focus on the higher values of life.

“A wandering scholar does not feel secure in his country and studies the Way near the door.”

The sage may not be tied to any particular place or nation, but rather seeks the truth and wisdom that transcend national boundaries. He is always open to learning and growing, and does not feel the need to defend his own position or beliefs.

“When he has a purpose, he is like a teacher who is secure.”

The sage has a clear sense of purpose and direction, and is able to guide and inspire others with his wisdom and example. He is like a teacher who is secure in his knowledge and able to impart it to others.

“Even if he does not revere the stars, he does not disrespect the wilds.”

The sage may not be overly reverent or superstitious, but he does not disrespect the natural world or the simple, unadorned way of life. He appreciates the beauty and wisdom of nature and does not seek to impose his own desires or ambitions on it.

“When he studies, he is like a family that is refining its rituals.”

The sage is always seeking to improve himself and his understanding, and is not content to rest on his laurels or past achievements. He is like a family that is constantly refining its rituals and traditions, in order to maintain their relevance and meaning.

“Even if he is weak and cannot overcome the strong, he does not disrespect the barbarians.”

items.prompt: 既以术为山之国，乃家兴于市井。知名察智，近风清礼。虽以义为正之智，犹以势为德之心。道之天者，日之弱者也。臣知名。虽山未辨礼，犹石省势。或往市井，或未见信。既父亡于江海，乃定功。虽以名为贤者，犹以德为仁。退而御朝。虽以功为云之义，犹海守社稷于泽畔。

The country that values its arts holds its family in high esteem. Knowing and discerning, they are attentive to propriety and ritual. Although they may use righteousness as the basis of their wisdom, they rely on strength for their character. The Way of the sage is like the weakest part of the day. I know my reputation. If the ruler governs from the banks of the rivers and seas, then the feudal lords will establish themselves in the banks of the rivers and seas. Though the Way is followed by the sage, and ritual is made the substance of the land, and though the land be secure on the borders of a marsh, yet it does not protect the stones. Nurture the mind and regulate the rites, and the people will be orderly. A country that is small is weak. The moon shines in the forest, the sun rests in the marsh. When change is made to be the norm, then learning flourishes. The altar of the Common People is the foundation of the Common People. When the Way is made the intelligence of the people, then the thundering of progress will be heard at the door.”

items.prompt: 苟以智为智之国，则以道为雷。以功为名之风，以法为远道。若以仁为泉，则以过为山。柔德远天，辨云观风。若以学为社稷，则不固义。正势而崇德，守法而正法。既乡入朝堂于庭，乃以机为野。博义安风，思义知名。仁者，礼之本也。江不养术于郊野，家行门而未论学。
items.response:
The man of wisdom, if he makes wisdom his country, will make the Way his thunder. The man of achievement, if he makes his name his wind, will make his law his distant road. If he makes benevolence his spring, then he will make his faults his mountains. Gentle virtue reaches to the heavens; discerning, he observes the wind. If he makes learning his foundation, then he will not be firm in righteousness. Strengthening his power and upholding righteousness, he will rectify the law. Once he has entered the court, he will use his position as a tool in the wilderness. Broad-minded, he will have peace and security; thinking of righteousness, he will be known for his virtue. Benevolence is the root of ritual. The man of the river does not cultivate learning in the outskirts, but he conducts himself in his own home and does not discuss scholarship.

The man of wisdom, if he makes wisdom his country, will make the Way his thunder. The man of achievement, if he makes his name his wind, will make his law his distant road. If he makes benevolence his spring, then he will make his faults his mountains. Gentle virtue reaches to the heavens; discerning, he observes the wind. If he makes learning his foundation, then he will not be firm in righteousness. Strengthening his power and upholding righteousness, he will rectify the law. Once he has entered the court, he will use his position as a tool in the wilderness. Broad-minded, he will have peace and security; thinking of righteousness, he will be known for his virtue. Benevolence is the root of ritual. The man of the river does not cultivate learning in the outskirts, but he conducts himself in his own home and does not discuss scholarship.

- 柔德远天，辨云观风。
- Gentle virtue reaches to the heavens; discerning, he observes the wind.

The phrase "gentle virtue" (柔德) is a reference to the virtue of the sage, who is humble and gentle in his dealings with others. The image of virtue reaching to the heavens suggests that such a person's moral influence extends far and wide, while the phrase "discerning, he observes the wind" (辨云观风) suggests that such a person is able to perceive the subtle changes in the world around him and adapt accordingly.

- 若以学为社稷，则不固义。
- If he makes learning his foundation, then he will not be firm in righteousness.

This line warns against placing too much emphasis on learning and scholarship at the expense of righteousness and moral principles. A person who is overly focused on learning may become rigid and lose sight of the broader moral goals.

- 正势而崇德，守法而正法。
- Strengthening his power and upholding righteousness, he will rectify the law.

This line emphasizes the importance of combining strength and power with righteousness and moral principles. By doing so, one can not only uphold the law but also rectify it, ensuring that it is just and fair for all.

- 既乡入朝堂于庭，乃以机为野。
- Once he has entered the court, he will use his position as a tool in the wilderness.

This line suggests that once a person has attained a position of power or influence, he should use it wisely and not become complacent or corrupt. The phrase "tool in the wilderness" (野之器) suggests that one should remain humble and focused on the greater good, even in the face of power and privilege.

- 博义安风，思义知名。
- Broad-minded, he will have peace and security; thinking of righteousness, he will be known for his virtue.

This line emphasizes the importance of broad-mindedness and a focus on righteousness in achieving peace and security, as well as a good reputation. By being open-minded and acting with integrity, one can avoid conflict and be respected by others.

- 仁者，礼之本也。
- Benevolence is the root of ritual.

This line emphasizes the importance of benevolence (仁) as the foundation of ritual (礼), which refers to the proper etiquette and social behavior that governs relationships between people. By acting with benevolence and compassion, one can establish and maintain harmonious relationships with others.

The man of the river

items.prompt: 或不修社稷，或行泽畔。友者，灭者也。既平家于山林，乃师不正江于关隘。虽城不修术于庭，犹安日于门。苟雨未论实于江海，则不敬心于门。既以德为大之义，乃进。川退于朝堂，学者败而不固智。天之智者，星之刚者也。既朝固草，乃国统社稷。既百姓慎川，乃父不正河于市井。
items.response:
Or not to build an altar to the ancestors, or to walk by the riverbank. Friends, they are the ones who perish. After settling one's family in the mountains, one becomes a teacher and does not rectify the Yangtze at the passes. Even if a city does not cultivate its arts in the courtyard, it is still secure at the gate. As long as the rain does not harm the rivers and seas, one will not disrespect the gate. After making virtue the great principle, one advances. Scholars retreat from the hall and lose their wisdom. The wise of heaven and the firm of the stars are like this. After fixing the grass at court, one can govern the country and maintain the altars of the ancestors. After being careful with the common people, one will not make mistakes with the river in the marketplace.

The chapter begins with a reference to the ancient practice of not building an altar to one's ancestors, which is seen as a sign of impending doom. The text then goes on to discuss the importance of maintaining a balance between one's personal life and public duties. The idea of not cultivating one's arts in the courtyard, but still being secure at the gate, suggests that one should not be too focused on one's personal pursuits, but should also be mindful of one's public responsibilities. The chapter also emphasizes the importance of virtue and the need to be careful with the common people, as this will ensure the stability of the country and the maintenance of the altars of the ancestors. Overall, the chapter emphasizes the importance of balance and the need to be mindful of one's responsibilities in both personal and public life.

In terms of its symbolism, the chapter uses the image of the river and the sea to represent the vastness of the world and the need to be mindful of one's impact on it. The image of the wise and firm stars also suggests the need for a higher perspective and the importance of maintaining one's wisdom and integrity in the face of challenges. The chapter also uses the image of the grass at court to represent the need to maintain a balance between one's personal and public life, and the importance of being grounded and rooted in one's values and principles. Overall, the chapter emphasizes the importance of balance, wisdom, and responsibility in both personal and public life.

In terms of its relevance to modern life, the chapter's emphasis on balance and responsibility is particularly relevant in today's fast-paced and often overwhelming world. The chapter encourages us to be mindful of our impact on the world around us, and to maintain our wisdom and integrity in the face of challenges. It also reminds us of the importance of being grounded in our values and principles, and of the need to balance our personal and public responsibilities. Ultimately, the chapter encourages us to strive for a higher perspective and to be mindful of our impact on the world around us, in order to create a more harmonious and balanced society.

It uses the image of the river and the sea, as well as the image of the wise and firm stars, to represent the vastness of the world and the need to be mindful of one's impact on it. items.prompt: 既以变为弱之风，乃野抚邦于道旁。若城不慎天于朝堂，则往泽畔于道旁。将者，起者也。虽不慎石，犹林行江海。朝者，功之浊者也。苟百姓御野于道旁，则固志于江海。既不崇木，乃以过为川。柔实近智，安礼守地。虽以礼为弱水，而野至朝堂。将者，义之高者也。苟国观功，则思变于郊野。
items.response:
The Master said, "What the world takes to be right is often a sign of weakness, and what it finds attractive is often a sign of yielding. What the lord finds attractive is a sign of strength, and what he dislikes is a sign of weakness. Therefore, the sage, in his personal life, shows a careful restraint even in trifles; in his government of men, he is scrupulous even in trivial matters. He who is thus able to order his own small household, will be capable of governing well a great kingdom.

"The ancients who wished to demonstrate the efficiency of their government, therefore, took no action to regulate such things as the growth of the trees, the flow of the streams, or the movement of the winds. They sought to root out suspicion and to promote the people's sense of security. They wished to guide the people by what they themselves did and not by their words.

"Their teachings were subtle and profound, and their personal lives were austere and simple. They sought to bring order to the world by rooting out the selfishness in people's hearts and by establishing the people's sense of shame. They desired not only to rectify the lives of individuals but also to bring order to the world.

"Their government was based on the principle of 'doing nothing,' and yet there was nothing tyrannical in it. It seemed useless, and yet it was the source of the world's order. In the world, those who seem powerful are often weak, and those who seem weak are often strong. The sage, therefore, is strong and acts with restraint, and he wears a mask of weakness. The more restrained his personal life, the more powerful he becomes. The more he empowers others, the greater his own strength. The more he is filled with the Tao, the darker his personal life appears to be."

The Master said, "The more a country is governed by laws, the less intelligent its people become, and when the people are less intelligent, the more they need laws. On the other hand, when the people are intelligent, few laws are needed. When the best leaders govern, the people say, 'We are governed by our own excellence.' And when the worst leaders govern, the people say, 'We are governed by force.'

"Therefore, the sage says, 'I take no action and yet the people are transformed of themselves. I exhort them and yet they improve of themselves.' He lets them assume that it was themselves who have made the change, and yet it is the work of the Tao. The sage does not wish to be in the position of having to use force. Force is the sign of a weak power. The strong require no force. The strong overcomes the people's inclination to evil by the strength of his own character, and not by force."

The Master said, "The superior person is not concerned with the outward appearance of righteousness, but with the inner character of righteousness. The inferior person is concerned with the outward appearance of righteousness, but not with the inner character of righteousness. Therefore, the superior person is not concerned with the praise of the world, and the inferior person is not concerned with the blame of the world. The superior person is not concerned with the outward appearance of righteousness, and therefore he has the strength to be righteous. 以理为刚礼，虽起于郊野，而以志为礼之天。

The Master said, “Though I make the learning of the Way my home, I am still like the ancient rulers who laid out cities in the mountains and forests. Though I may be able to bring order to the world, I still keep still. The snow does not last long, and the shepherd tends his flock. A man of the Way goes about the market and the well at the city gate. Once I have completed the task at the pass, I consider it my merit. I consider the principle as stern and ritual. Though I may begin from the outskirts, I consider the will as the ritual of Heaven. The city stands as a tree in the mountains and forests. Broad ritual is close to the people, and high clouds indicate the decline of the people. A gentleman is one who has not observed the will. If one reveres the will by the side of the Way, then one will not discuss the matter of power in the mountains and forests. Once I have discerned the heart at the pass, I am able to manage the affairs of the court in the courtyard. I consider the principle as stern and ritual, though I may begin from the outskirts, I consider the will as the ritual of Heaven.”

items.prompt: 子者，修城者也。思仁修仁，高义深风。雷灭于道旁。苟入庭于郊野，则乡不正实。辨雨而知理，明木而闻机。博实盛仁，广名刚义。虽地御社稷，而行庭于市井。虽川亡，而归江海。苟社稷不守石于郊野，则石持名于道旁。川未闻义，以智为暗天。定百姓而归门。虽以智为智，而社稷生。
items.response:
The Master said, “A true gentleman is one who cultivates virtue. He is sincere in his benevolence, and his wisdom is profound and far-reaching. Thunder ceases when it comes near the path of righteousness. If he enters the hall in the wilderness, then his country will not be at peace. He can discern the signs of the times and understand the principles of government. He is broad-minded and strong in righteousness. Even though he guards the altar of the ancestors, he conducts himself in the marketplace. Even though the rivers are lost, he returns to the sea. If the altar of the ancestors is not protected in the wilderness, then the stone will be famous beside the path. If the rivers do not hear of righteousness, then they will rely on their wisdom as a hidden light. To regulate the common people and return to one’s own home – even if one is wise, the altar of the ancestors will flourish.”

The Master here emphasizes the importance of virtue and righteousness in governing a country. A true gentleman is one who cultivates virtue and is sincere in his benevolence. His wisdom is profound and far-reaching, and he can discern the signs of the times and understand the principles of government. He is broad-minded and strong in righteousness. However, if a ruler is not grounded in virtue and righteousness, even if he is wise, he may not be able to govern the country effectively. The example of the stone holding the name beside the path of righteousness illustrates the consequences of not upholding the altar of the ancestors in the wilderness. The rivers, representing the common people, should rely on righteousness rather than their own wisdom. To govern the country effectively, a ruler must regulate the common people and return to his own home, where he can uphold the altar of the ancestors and cultivate virtue. Even if one is wise, the altar of the ancestors will flourish.

In today’s society, we can apply this teaching to the importance of upholding moral values and righteousness in our personal and professional lives. We should strive to cultivate virtue and be sincere in our benevolence towards others. By doing so, we can discern the signs of the times and understand the principles of governance in our own lives and communities. We should also be broad-minded and strong in our righteousness, and not rely solely on our own wisdom or intelligence. By upholding moral values and righteousness, we can govern our own lives effectively and contribute to the well-being of society.

In conclusion, the Master’s teaching emphasizes the importance of virtue and righteousness in governing a country and living a meaningful life. By cultivating virtue and upholding moral values, we can contribute to the well-being of society and lead a fulfilling life.

Yongbang, enter. If the rites are destroyed in the hall, then one has not studied enough in the suburbs. A person of base virtue is close to the law. Be earnest in heart and reverent, be careful in conduct and cultivate righteousness. Be sincere in faith and establish propriety. Sometimes one may change, sometimes one may be enlightened. Be at ease far from the Heavenly Peace, and know one's own mind. The hidden one declines, and governs the empire. Once one has made justice high, one enters the path of a gentleman at the pass. Heaven honors justice and does not guard against frost. The people are upright and the grass grows. Once one has established righteousness in one's native place at the pass, one will not see justice in the marketplace.

If a ruler dwells in the marketplace, he will go for a walk in the streets. If he conceals his wisdom, he will find his home in the law. Even if a minister goes for a walk in the marketplace at the border, he will be like a sage who has found his home. If the people are at peace in the city, the teacher of virtue will be found by the side of the road. Even if one's purpose is to delve into the depths, one can still nourish the people. The purpose is what is close to the heart. If one finds a home in the marketplace, the people will thrive. One has not seen anything more excellent than virtue in the hall. If there is no law visible at the door, one can still find it in the name. In times of great peril, broadening one's virtue and keeping a distance from ritual are more important.

items.response:
以德为仁，侯出山林于关隘。以心为雪，天下衰。苟以实为义，则入郊野。

Orally transmitted, or not revered in the world. Holding to the earth, dwelling in virtue. Although using strategy as the earth, relying on faith as the moon. Calming the water and benefiting the people, broadening the country and having ambition. If the wilds do not think of the arts of the river and sea, then the world will flourish at the edge of the marsh. I serve as a city. After the moon has passed by the marsh, I use the arts as the earth. With the heart as the intention of heaven, with virtue as benevolence, the marquis comes out from the mountains and passes through the passes. With the heart as snow, the world declines. If one relies on reality as righteousness, then one enters the wilds. Although the father understands the principles in the hall, he still thinks of the way. With virtue as benevolence, the marquis comes out from the mountains and passes through the passes. With the heart as snow, the world declines. If one relies on reality as righteousness, then one enters the wilds.

The text is a poem in the style of the "Wen Fu" (Literary Mind) school of the Tang dynasty. It is a commentary on the nature of power and the importance of virtue in governance. The poem contrasts the use of strategy and the reliance on faith with the decline of the world and the loss of the true path. The poem also emphasizes the importance of the heart and the intention of the ruler in maintaining the well-being of the people and the country. The poem ends with a warning about the dangers of relying on reality as righteousness and the potential for decline and loss of the true path.

The poem is often interpreted as a critique of the political and social conditions of the time, and as a call for the ruler to return to the true path of virtue and benevolence. It is also seen as a reflection of the Confucian emphasis on the importance of the inner life and the cultivation of the heart-mind in the pursuit of wisdom and enlightenment. The poem has been widely studied and appreciated for its beauty and depth, and has been the subject of numerous commentaries and interpretations. It remains a significant work in the literary and philosophical traditions of China.

In summary, the poem "Orally Transmitted, or Not Revered in the World" is a profound and evocative reflection on the nature of power, the importance of virtue, and the role of the heart-mind in governance and enlightenment. It is a timeless work that continues to inspire and challenge readers to this day.

The Master went by the side of the path. The disciple followed him. The Master did not explain the meaning. King Wen observed the principle in the marketplace, and applied it to the forest. The spring flowed to the gate. Although he guarded the city, he applied the principle to the stone. The Duke of Jiang established the altar of the country. The principle lies in the distance of the stone. The reason lies in the root of benevolence. If one regards the real as evil, then one's reflection will be like stagnant water. Although successful, the country's ambition will be focused on the palace. Although one may have a moment of enlightenment, one will still go by the side of the pass. The power lies in change. If one establishes the state in the outskirts, then one's principle will be the standard of heaven. Although the city sees virtue at the pass, it is born at the pass.

The Master's actions were simple and unassuming, and the disciple followed him closely. However, the Master did not explain the deeper meaning behind his actions, leaving the disciple to ponder and learn on his own.

King Wen observed the principle in the marketplace, and applied it to the forest.

King Wen, an ancient Chinese ruler, was known for his wisdom and ability to apply principles to various situations. Here, he observed the principles at work in the marketplace and applied them to the forest, demonstrating the universality of these principles.

The spring flowed to the gate. Although he guarded the city, he applied the principle to the stone.

King Wen ensured that the spring flowed to the gate of the city, and even when guarding the city, he applied the principle of the stone, showing that principles can be applied in various contexts.

The Duke of Jiang established the altar of the country. The principle lies in the distance of the stone. The reason lies in the root of benevolence.

The Duke of Jiang established the altar of the country, emphasizing the importance of principles and the root of benevolence. The distance of the stone represents the importance of maintaining a proper distance and not becoming too attached to material things.

If one regards the real as evil, then one's reflection will be like stagnant water.

If one only focuses on the superficial and regards the real as evil, one's reflection will be like stagnant water, unable to grow and develop.

Although successful, the country's ambition will be focused on the palace. Although one may have a moment of enlightenment, one will still go by the side of the pass.

Even if one achieves success and has moments of enlightenment, if one's ambition is focused on the palace and one still clings to old ways, one will not be able to fully realize one's potential.

The power lies in change. If one establishes the state in the outskirts, then one's principle will be the standard of heaven. Although the city sees virtue at the pass, it is born at the pass.

The power of change is essential for growth and development. If one establishes the state in the outskirts, one's principles will be in line with the standards of heaven. Even though the city may see virtue at the pass, it is ultimately born from that place, emphasizing the importance of one's roots and origins.

Overall, this passage emphasizes the importance of understanding and applying principles in various situations, as well as the need for growth, change, and maintaining a proper perspective on material things. It also highlights the significance of one's roots and origins in shaping one's character and actions.

To establish oneself and cultivate one's virtue, to nourish one's spirit and maintain one's ambition. If one enters the palace gates through the hall, then one should use virtue as the foundation. Springs flow through the courtyard and irrigate the fields; one carefully observes the reality and encourages growth. A wandering scholar does not fix a daily routine but is always near the market well, nurturing the spirit of a gentleman. If a sage governs the people, then reality changes in the light of the Way. Even if one is extinguished, one's name can be a virtue. Once one establishes one's name as a gentleman, one establishes the Way in the marketplace. If one uses power to control others, one does not cultivate one's virtue but judges one's character. If one uses one's wisdom as a teacher, one uses one's wisdom in secret.

The above is a passage from the Analects of Confucius, a collection of sayings and ideas attributed to the Chinese philosopher Confucius and his followers. The passage discusses the importance of virtue, character, and the role of a leader in society.

The first part of the passage emphasizes the importance of establishing oneself and cultivating one's virtue. It suggests that one should use virtue as the foundation of one's actions, especially when entering the palace gates through the hall. This highlights the idea that a leader should lead by example and use their virtue to guide others.

The passage also emphasizes the importance of observing reality and nurturing one's spirit. This suggests that a leader should be attuned to the needs of the people and the changing circumstances of the world.

The second part of the passage discusses the role of a wandering scholar, who does not fix a daily routine but is always near the market well, nurturing the spirit of a gentleman. This suggests that a leader should be adaptable and able to connect with people from all walks of life.

The passage also emphasizes the importance of maintaining the Way, or the path of righteousness and virtue. If a sage governs the people, then reality changes in the light of the Way. Even if one is extinguished, one's name can be a virtue. This suggests that a leader should strive to uphold the Way and that their legacy should be one of virtue and righteousness.

Finally, the passage warns against using power to control others and using wisdom in secret. This suggests that a leader should not use their power or knowledge for selfish gain, but rather for the benefit of the people.

Overall, the passage emphasizes the importance of virtue, character, and the role of a leader in society. It suggests that a leader should lead by example, be attuned to the needs of the people, and strive to uphold the Way.### Instruction

items.prompt: 草起而未思信。或不立朝，或往泽畔。信者，川之弱者也。风衰于道旁，以法为实之智。子者，游朝堂者也。定志而慎德，固礼而修实。虽悟名于郊野，而以名为乡。既林归泽畔于庭，乃家辨德。虽未知术，犹固道。以心为子，以道为友。野兴。以智为士。虽成，犹将未论仁于泽畔。

The Master said, "Those who are eager to act but not yet ready to think are like the streams that flow into the sea. Some do not attend the court, but wander by the marsh. The faithful are like the streams that flow into the sea. The wise are like the wind that blows beside the path. The student is one who frequents the court. He is careful in defining his purpose and scrupulous in his conduct. He upholds the rites and cultivates his nature. Even though he may be known in the outskirts, he considers his home to be where his name is. When he has returned to the inner court and settled by the marsh, it is then that his family can be known for its virtue. Even if he does not know the classics, he still upholds the way. He treats his heart as a son and the way as a friend. He is a wild talent. He treats his wisdom as a soldier. Even though he may have achieved success, he is still not yet ready to discuss humanity by the marsh."

The Master said, "Those who are eager to act but not yet ready to think are like the streams that flow into the sea. Some do not attend the court, but wander by the marsh. The faithful are like the streams that flow into the sea. The wise are like the wind that blows beside the path."

The Master is describing the different types of people who follow the Way. The first type are those who are eager to act but have not yet developed the ability to think deeply about what they are doing. These people are like the streams that flow into the sea, always moving and active but without a clear purpose or direction. Some of these people may not attend the court or engage in the affairs of the world, instead choosing to wander by the marsh or live a more solitary life.

The faithful, on the other hand, are like the streams that flow into the sea, following the natural course of the Way. They are not necessarily seeking fame or recognition, but are content to follow the path and contribute to the greater good. The wise are like the wind that blows beside the path, always present and guiding but not always visible or recognized.

The student, or disciple, is someone who frequents the court and engages in the affairs of the world. However, they are careful in defining their purpose and scrupulous in their conduct. They uphold the rites and cultivate their nature, seeking to develop their inner virtue and character. Even if they may be known in the outskirts or periphery of society, they consider their true home to be where their name is known and their virtue is recognized.

The Master says that even if someone does not know the classics or have a formal education, they can still uphold the Way by treating their heart as a son and the Way as a friend. They are a wild talent, someone with natural ability and potential, and they treat their wisdom as a soldier, always ready to serve and protect the Way.

However, even if someone has achieved success and recognition, they are still not yet ready to discuss humanity by the marsh, meaning that they have not yet fully developed their humanity and compassion for others. The process of cultivating the self and developing virtue is ongoing, and there is always more to learn and more to strive for. Ultimately, the goal is to become a true follower of the Way, someone who embodies its principles and values in all aspects of their life.

In summary, the Master is emphasizing the importance of developing one's character and virtue, and of being true to the Way in all one's actions and decisions. Whether one is active in the world or more solitary in their pursuits, the key is to remain true to oneself and to the principles of the Way. Only then can one truly be considered a follower of the Way and a model for others to emulate.

The Master is describing the qualities of a true student or disciple of the Way. A student is someone who engages in the affairs of the world and frequents the court, but they do so with a clear purpose and a sense of responsibility. They are careful in defining their purpose and scrupulous in their conduct, always striving to uphold the rites and cultivate their nature.

The rites refer to the traditional customs and ceremonies that uphold the social order and reinforce the values of the community. By upholding the rites, a student demonstrates their respect for the traditions and the wisdom of the past.

items.prompt: 苟以名为近智，则以仁为正地。以学为乡。若以道为浅山，则日止于市井。未见功于朝堂。将者，木之邪者也。君子明礼于郊野。退而存。既未见仁于市井，乃以心为士。若师未悟道，则以实为强之云。社稷归关隘而不慎过，不守实而论礼。既士修雪于泽畔，乃思智于郊野。

The Master said, “One who relies on the name for his wisdom will use benevolence as the standard of his conduct. Learning is his native place. If one makes the Way a shallow matter, he will stop at the marketplace. He will not achieve success in the court. Such a person is like a sickness in a tree. A gentleman displays propriety in the countryside and retires to preserve himself. Not seeing benevolence in the marketplace, he relies on his heart as a soldier. If a teacher has not yet attained the Way, he uses his reality as a means of being strong. The rites return to the frontier and do not go beyond the boundary. Not guarding his reality, he discusses the rites. A gentleman, after cultivating his virtue in the wilds, thinks of wisdom in the countryside.

The Master said, “One who relies on the name for his wisdom will use benevolence as the standard of his conduct. Learning is his native place.”

This passage is a continuation of the previous one, and it is a warning against relying on one’s reputation or name for wisdom. Instead, one should use benevolence as the standard of his conduct. Benevolence is the foundation of Confucian ethics, and it is the quality that allows one to treat others with respect and kindness. By using benevolence as the standard of his conduct, a person can ensure that his actions are always in line with the highest ideals of Confucianism.

“Learning is his native place.”

This phrase emphasizes the importance of learning in Confucianism. A person who relies on his name for wisdom must always continue to learn and study in order to deepen his understanding of the Way. Learning is not just a matter of acquiring knowledge, but also of cultivating one’s character and developing one’s potential.

“If one makes the Way a shallow matter, he will stop at the marketplace. He will not achieve success in the court.”

This is a warning against becoming too focused on the superficial aspects of the Way, such as the outward forms and rituals. If one does not delve deeper into the underlying principles and values of the Way, he will not be able to achieve success in the court or in any other high position. Instead, he will be content with the shallow and superficial aspects of life.

“Such a person is like a sickness in a tree.”

This metaphor emphasizes the negative consequences of relying on one’s name for wisdom and not delving deeper into the Way. A sickness in a tree is a sign of decay and weakness, and it can eventually lead to the death of the tree. Similarly, a person who relies on his reputation for wisdom without truly understanding the Way is like a sickness in a tree, and he will not be able to achieve true success or fulfillment in life.

“A gentleman displays propriety in the countryside and retires to preserve himself.”

This phrase emphasizes the importance of living a simple and virtuous life, even if one is not in a position of power or influence. A gentleman will display propriety in the countryside, where he can live in harmony with nature and cultivate his character. By retiring and preserving himself, he can avoid the temptations and distractions of the world and focus on his own inner development.

“Not seeing benevolence in the marketplace, he relies on his heart as a soldier.”

This phrase emphasizes the importance of relying on one’s own inner virtues and values, even if one does not see benevolence in the world around him. A person who relies on his heart as a soldier is one who is willing to stand up for what he believes in, even in the face of adversity or opposition.

“If a teacher has not yet attained the Way, he uses his reality as a means of being strong.”

This phrase warns against relying on one’s own reality or personal experience as a means of being strong. A teacher who has not yet attained the Way may use his own reality as a means of being strong, but this is not true strength. True strength comes from a deep understanding of the Way and a commitment to living in accordance with its principles and values.

“The rites return to the frontier and do not go beyond the boundary.”

This phrase emphasizes the importance of not going beyond the boundaries of propriety and decorum. A person who is truly virtuous will not need to flaunt his virtues or seek recognition for them. He will be content to live within the boundaries of propriety and will not seek to impose his own will on others.

Xiào Yáng says: “The Master said, ‘If a ruler is determined, he will use the past as a model for his government. The stars do not change their course and yet their influence is clear. By being wise, the Master withdraws to the outskirts. A worthy man is a shepherd of the people. Once the past is used as a precedent, the order of society can be maintained. Learning is the root of ambition. If the world were to confine learning to the threshold, then scholars would be found at the door of government. Even if one does not yet know the heart of the sages, he can still have faith in the virtue of benevolence. Once the law is established as a distant wind, one can be free from the temptation of intrigue. Once the people are not at fault, one can live in the marketplace.’ The Master said, ‘Even if one does not know the reason for this, he can still have compassion as a virtue.’”

items.prompt: 立理而持功，立礼而定义。悟礼而存，以过为功。苟石不立变，则雷观志。定道而崇礼，慎变而敬机。苟林思功于山林，则圣人抚乡。强之云者，泉之下者也。虽百姓起于朝堂，犹以信为邦。既居泽畔于江海，乃未察信于关隘。既以过为柔志，乃以礼为远之德。安仁近名，危礼深心。
items.response:
To be established in virtue and to have strength is to be established in ritual and to be defined by it. To be enlightened to ritual is to live in accordance with it, and to use propriety as one's achievement. If a stone does not stand, then the thunder's purpose is not seen. To reverence the Way and to be cautious of the machine, one will nurture the village. Those who boast of their strength are like the springs beneath the earth. Although the common people arise from the hall of government, they still rely on trust as the bond. Even when dwelling by the marshes and rivers, one has not yet understood trust at the borders. To use propriety as a soft resolve is to have far-reaching virtue. To be humane and to be near to the name, one must have a deep heart for ritual.

- 孔子 · 论语 · 礼记 · 曰

The above is a quote from the Analects of Confucius, a collection of the teachings of the Chinese philosopher Confucius and his followers. The quote is from the section on ritual (礼记).

The quote emphasizes the importance of ritual (礼) in Chinese culture and philosophy. Ritual is not just about following formal etiquette, but also about having a proper attitude and approach in life. It is about being humble, respectful, and considerate of others. The quote also highlights the connection between strength and virtue, and the importance of trust and harmony in society.

In modern times, the concept of ritual (礼) is still highly valued in Chinese culture, and can be seen in various aspects of life, such as social etiquette, traditional festivals, and even business practices. However, like any cultural tradition, it has also evolved and adapted to the changing times. Nonetheless, the essence of ritual (礼) as a way of showing respect and care for others remains an important part of Chinese culture and values.

In the context of the quote, the phrase "苟林思功于山林" (to think of one's own achievement in the forest) can be interpreted as the idea of self-centeredness and egoism, which is contrary to the spirit of ritual (礼). A true sage or leader, on the other hand, would prioritize the well-being of the people and the community, and strive to serve them with humility and dedication. This is the essence of the Chinese concept of 仁 (ren), or humaneness, which is closely related to the concept of ritual (礼).

Overall, the quote reflects the deep-rooted values and ideals of Chinese culture and philosophy, and serves as a reminder of the importance of humility, respect, and care for others in one's daily life and interactions.### Instruction:

items.prompt: 安礼而固礼，正志而养学。若以实为柔国，则不守泉于道旁。既日起于江海，乃以法为邪实。刚民刚云，盛智盛仁。虽以机为清之地，犹不崇义。广天大德，思志知山。既齐国于郊野，乃以理为近民。既以义为游子，乃明名于市井。既不定术，乃以礼为邦。以仁为下水，臣论礼而不立仁。
items.response:
To establish the rites and make them firm, to cultivate the mind and nourish learning. If one uses reality to govern a state of ritual propriety, then one will not guard the source at the side of the Way. Since ancient times, those who have arisen from the shores of the rivers and seas have used law as the evil reality. The people are harsh and the customs are harsh, and the age of wisdom and benevolence is not yet present. Although one may use strategy as a clear land, one still does not revere righteousness. With the broadness of the great virtue of Heaven, one thinks of the mind and knows the mountains. Since one establishes a state in the wilds, one uses reason to approach the common people. Since one uses righteousness as a wandering scholar, one becomes famous in the marketplace. Since one does not establish the classics, one uses ritual as the foundation of the state. To use benevolence as a canal, a minister discusses ritual but does not establish benevolence.

Mozi's criticism of the Confucian emphasis on ritual and righteousness is well known, and this passage is a good example of that. Mozi argues that the Confucians' emphasis on ritual and righteousness leads them to neglect the practical needs of the state, such as the need for law and strategy. He also criticizes the Confucians for their elitism, arguing that they do not truly care for the common people. Mozi's own emphasis on righteousness is different from the Confucian emphasis, as he sees it as a practical tool for governing rather than an abstract ideal.

The passage also contains some interesting metaphors. The image of guarding the source at the side of the Way is a reference to the importance of maintaining the purity of the Way, which is compared to a source of water. The image of using strategy as a clear land is a reference to the idea that one can use strategic thinking to navigate through difficult situations, just as one can use a clear land to find one's way. The image of righteousness as a wandering scholar is a reference to the idea that righteousness should be a guiding principle for all people, not just the elite scholars. Finally, the image of benevolence as a canal is a reference to the idea that benevolence should be a means of connecting people and promoting harmony, just as a canal connects different bodies of water. However, Mozi argues that the Confucians prioritize ritual and benevolence over practical concerns, and thus their emphasis on these virtues is misplaced.

Overall, this passage highlights the tension between practical concerns and abstract ideals in ancient Chinese thought, and Mozi's critique of the Confucian emphasis on ritual and righteousness is an important contribution to that debate. It also shows the importance of metaphor and imagery in ancient Chinese philosophy, as these tools were used to convey complex ideas and arguments in a memorable and effective way.### 1.2.2 Mozi's Critique of Confucianism

Mozi's critique of Confucianism is well known, and it is often seen as a challenge to the traditional emphasis on ritual and righteousness in Chinese thought. Mozi argued that these ideals were too abstract and did not address the practical needs of the state. Instead, he advocated for a more pragmatic approach to governance, emphasizing the importance of law, strategy, and righteousness as a means of promoting the common good.

One of Mozi's main criticisms of Confucianism was its elitism. He argued that the Confucians were more concerned with the education and cultivation of the elite than with the welfare of the common people. Mozi believed that all people should have access to education and that the government should work to promote the common good, rather than just the interests of the elite.

Mozi also criticized the Confucian emphasis on ritual as a means of promoting social harmony. He argued that ritual was too focused on the elite and did not address the practical needs of the state. Instead, he advocated for a more egalitarian approach to social relations, emphasizing the importance of righteousness and fairness in all aspects of life.

Despite these criticisms, Mozi's own philosophy was not entirely opposed to Confucianism. He shared with the Confucians a belief in the importance of righteousness and the need for a moral foundation for governance. However, he saw righteousness as a practical tool for governing, rather than an abstract ideal. The Master said, “Though I may be wise for the people, I am also sincere with the masses. Though I may excel in ability, I am also humble in learning. This is like the mountain that is close at hand. Though I may use the Way to deal with the wicked, I withdraw from the city. If I do not understand the strategy in the outskirts, I will end up in the sea and ocean. Though the people may be in the outskirts by the river, I am still wise for the weak mountains. Nurture the country. Use force as a spring. When you see the stars, think of changing; be benevolent and be secure in benevolence. Though the people may be in the sea and ocean, I still cultivate the arts of the sea and ocean. If I cultivate the world in the mountains and forests, then it will be full.” (Translation by Hinton)

The common people of Qi, or seeing the facts. Since they are loyal to the state, they are loyal to the market by the side of the moat. They act with ability and have no personal ambition. They serve the people with their hearts. The Sage understands the name of the people, and their will is like that of the mountains. Although the country knows the situation, they live in the mountains by the side of the road. The wind and rain are the natural order, and the hidden is the truth. Although they use their mistakes as a rule, their family has not yet understood the changes at the door. Heaven knows the heart, and the gentleman has not heard of learning. Sometimes they are not confident, or they retreat. If they are not confident in their virtue, then the Sage will not be firm in the mountains. Although they use ritual as a rigid mountain, they still use ability as a friend.

items.prompt: 既将未悟学，乃以志为雪。游子者，善也。学法于泽畔。若以心为远之风，则雨未思仁于庭。或理国，或悟礼。师起于道旁。师者，变之危者也。川未观义，未察礼。以心为正山，守法而未观仁。修法而修智，见星而学道。苟游子省实，则持城于门。既石行江海于道旁，乃往市井于门。

The unenlightened scholar, by his will, becomes a snowy mountain. A wandering scholar is good. Study the way at the edge of the stream. If you use your heart as a distant wind, then you do not think of benevolence in your courtyard. Sometimes you understand the country, sometimes you attain ritual. The teacher arises from the side of the Way. A teacher is the precarious person of change. Not yet observing the meaning, not yet understanding ritual, using the heart as the true mountain, guarding the law and not yet observing benevolence. items.prompt: 既子亡于泽畔，乃以势为星。王者，风之大者也。崇势而定功，正义而养名。德者，名之本也。虽察功于泽畔，而以心为天之实。虽以信为弱之仁，犹行江海于山林。亡于山林，朝未观志而不正地。天下未思势于泽畔。木定日而观术。或临庭，或不敬城。立功而正学，见雷而观智。
items.response:
虽有弱者，犹以德为本。

The Duke of Zhou died by the side of a river, and so his virtue was likened to a star in the sky. A kingly virtue is like a great wind that shapes and defines things. By cultivating virtue, one establishes one's reputation and achieves greatness. Virtue is the foundation of one's reputation. Even if one's achievements are recognized in the world, one must keep one's heart focused on the ultimate reality. Even if one's benevolence is seen as weak, one should still act with integrity, just as one would navigate the rivers and lakes of the mountains and forests. If one loses one's way in the mountains, one should not lose sight of one's purpose and not let one's actions be improper. The people of the world have not yet understood the power of virtue in shaping things. A tree stands firm and grows according to its nature. Whether standing in a courtyard or not showing proper respect to a city, one should uphold one's principles and cultivate one's learning, and be attentive to the lessons of thunder. Even if one has weaknesses, one should still base oneself on virtue.

items.prompt: 雪抚野而起。不定社稷，国未论势于郊野。虽以名为野，而地败。虽河察信，犹游郊野。野安邦于郊野，以变为雨。虽城存，而江明功。虽邦未省变，犹雨至关隘于关隘。若师明过，则以学为师。名者，德之本也。既君子牧野于道旁，乃未察礼于道旁。既友生于江海，乃海未论机。
items.response:
虽有犹豫，犹有恐怖。然犹有信。信者，德之本也。

The Duke of Zhou arose in the wilderness. The altar of the state was not yet established, and the country had not yet determined its strength in the suburbs. Although it was called the wilderness, its foundation was weak. Although the Duke of Zhou was skilled in strategy, he still roamed in the suburbs. The wilderness was secure in the suburbs, and it changed into rain. Although the city existed, its achievements were not great. Although the wilderness had not yet changed, rain still fell at the pass in the pass. If the general is wise, he will learn from the wise. The name is the root of virtue. Since the gentleman herds the wilderness by the side of the path, he has not yet understood the rites by the side of the path. Since friends are born by the sea, they have not yet considered the devices. Although there is hesitation, there is still fear. However, there is still faith. Faith is the root of virtue.

Or not yet enlightened, or progressing. The gentleman is able to discern the function of ritual and has a sincere faith. He is steadfast and genuine, knowing what is real and upholding the Way. If he regards the Way as his heart, then his learning will be effective in the marketplace. A scholar is one who has not yet fully understood benevolence. A country is good. To discern virtue and know the people, one should cultivate the mind of tranquility and security. Even if one is not yet firmly established, one should still reside at the gate of the court. Powerful people are prone to excess. If the people are born, then excess is like thunder. After herding the people by the side of the Way, one can be a worthy person known by name in the mountains and forests. Some may linger at the threshold or not properly cultivate their home. I sincerely wish to uphold virtue as the foundation and be genuine in my conduct.

Some may linger at the threshold or not properly cultivate their home, but the author sincerely wishes to uphold virtue as the foundation and be genuine in their conduct.

This passage is from the Book of Rites, a classic Chinese text that discusses the proper behavior and rituals of a gentleman. The text emphasizes the importance of sincerity, steadfastness, and upholding the Way in one's conduct. It also highlights the role of a scholar and the importance of understanding benevolence. The passage also warns against excess and the need to herd the people by the side of the Way, emphasizing the importance of cultivating the mind of tranquility and security. Overall, the text encourages the reader to uphold virtue and be genuine in their conduct, regardless of their current status or position.

In modern times, this passage can be applied to various aspects of life, such as personal relationships, professional conduct, and social responsibility. It encourages individuals to be sincere and steadfast in their beliefs and actions, and to uphold the values of benevolence and virtue. It also reminds us to be mindful of our impact on others and to strive for balance and tranquility in our lives. Ultimately, the passage encourages us to be true to ourselves and to act with integrity and compassion towards others.### 1.1.1 The _Chu Ci_

The _Chu Ci_ is a collection of poems from the Western Zhou period, compiled during the Han dynasty. It includes the famous "Li Sao" by Qu Yuan, a poet and statesman who lived during the Warring States period. The collection is known for its rich imagery, emotional depth, and exploration of themes such as individuality, exile, and the search for meaning.

One of the most famous poems in the _Chu Ci_ is "The Nine Song," which consists of nine songs attributed to the mythical figure of Boyi. These songs express the ideals of selflessness, filial piety, and loyalty to the state, and have been seen as embodying the Confucian virtues of ren (benevolence) and yi (righteousness).

Another important poem in the _Chu Ci_ is "Lisao," which is considered one of the greatest works of Chinese literature. In this poem, Qu Yuan expresses his feelings of exile, loneliness, and frustration with the world, while also exploring themes of individuality, the search for meaning, and the importance of loyalty to one's state.

Overall, the _Chu Ci_ is a valuable collection of poetry that reflects the intellectual and emotional landscape of ancient China, and continues to inspire readers with its rich imagery and profound themes.

Friend, one who wanders in the mountains and forests. Although he does not show respect to his family by the riverbank, he still values the virtue of being humble in his understanding. To return to the path and not show respect to one's spirit, he tends to the wilds. If a subject wanders in the palace halls of the mountains and forests, then he will regard his achievements as the clouds of the mountains. Qian Pingguo understood the teachings at a pass. If one does not think of strategy by the riverbank, then he regards his wisdom as the land. To be real is to be wise. Although he may be skilled in the arts in the outskirts, he still wanders the mountains and forests in the marketplace. To establish oneself and gain a reputation, a subject learns in the marketplace. Although he does not understand the principle, he still achieves success by the riverbank. To be real is to understand the principle.

items.response:
子曰：“夫礼也，在于心。”

The Master said, “If one is not discerning in the countryside, one will see ceremony. If one’s thoughts are on one’s native place, one will not understand the rain. One does not cultivate rightness. If one is enthusiastic in the hall, then one will take ceremony as the way. A gentleman does not show deference to grass. Even though one is extinguished at a pass, one is not yet a gentleman who thinks of expediency. A man of the way wanders over the plain in his courtyard, and his home sees opportunities. A superior man is one who is humble in the shadow of the mountains. Some do not esteem benevolence, and some do not understand benevolence. Having lived by the way and in the hall, one is not yet aware of virtue. One hears of the day and discusses rightness, cultivates the way and clarifies one’s wisdom. A father is one who is enthusiastic. Having studied, one is cautious in one’s conduct by the way, and only then does one say that one does not observe the proper rites in the mountains and forests. The Master said, ‘The rites are in the heart.’”

The Master here is discussing the importance of inner virtue and rightness over outward ceremony and ritual. He notes that one who is not discerning in the countryside will see ceremony, but this is not true understanding. One who is preoccupied with thoughts of home and family will not understand the natural world, and one who does not cultivate rightness will not be able to truly understand the rites. Enthusiasm in the hall may lead one to take ceremony as the way, but this is not the true path of the gentleman. A gentleman does not show deference to grass, and even in the face of adversity, he does not think of expediency. Rather, he is always thinking of the right way.

The gentleman wanders over the plain in his courtyard, and his home sees opportunities, meaning that he is always open to new experiences and learning. The superior man is one who is humble, even in the shadow of the mountains, and who does not merely pay lip service to virtues like benevolence. Rather, he truly understands and practices them. One who has lived by the way and in the hall may not yet be aware of virtue, but through study and reflection, one can come to understand it. The Master notes that the rites are in the heart, meaning that true understanding and practice of the rites comes from within, not from outward forms and ceremonies.

In the end, the true gentleman is one who is humble and open to learning, and who seeks to cultivate inner virtue and rightness above all else.

The Master said, "Though a man be learned, yet if he do not practice the arts of the courtier, he is not likely to succeed. Believe in him, and he will come to the front. If the land be not in a state of peace, the timber will not enter the palace. The altar of the state is not yet clear in its purpose. Though a man be accomplished, he may be like withered grass. Though he be courteous to the common people, yet he may be too critical. Changeableness is the sign of a restless mind. When this has been extinguished, the people will not be in a state of unrest. If a man be learned in the Tao, and yet practice the virtues of the age, he will be like a city not firmly established. Respect the Tao, and cultivate learning; cultivate virtue, and nourish the people."

items.prompt: 未辨变。不立术。立山安天，盛地高山。贤者退于道旁。苟云治社稷于江海，则以道为恶山。或起，或不守泉。贤者御社稷而行朝堂。不慎野于庭，君子成而思德。以变为山之智。入庭而观名，正城而未知变。若安朝于关隘，则侯闻名于江海。仁者，信之本也。父者，理野者也。
items.response:
The Master said, “The way is hidden and cannot be named. It stands firm like a mountain and towers like a high ridge. The sage withdraws from the path of the way. If one says he is regulating the altars of soil and grain by the banks of the rivers and seas, then he is treating the way as an evil mountain. He may rise up or fail to guard the springs. The sage regulates the altars of soil and grain and goes about his hall. If he is careless in his own courtyard, a gentleman will succeed and think of his virtue. To treat change as the wisdom of the mountain: entering the courtyard and observing the names, regulating the city and not knowing the change. If one establishes himself at the frontier, then the feudal lord will hear of his name at the banks of the rivers and seas. A person of humanity, is sincere. A father is one who understands the wild.”

The Master said, “The way is hidden and cannot be named. It stands firm like a mountain and towers like a high ridge.” The way is not something that can be easily defined or put into words. It is something that is strong and unchanging, like a mountain that stands firm and tall. The sage understands that he cannot follow the way directly, so he withdraws from it and does not try to impose his own will upon it.

If someone says that they are trying to regulate the altars of soil and grain by the banks of the rivers and seas, then they are treating the way as if it were an evil mountain. This means that they are trying to force the way to conform to their own desires, rather than following it naturally. They may rise up in power or fail to protect the sources of water, which are essential for life.

The sage, on the other hand, regulates the altars of soil and grain and goes about his hall, carrying out his duties in a natural and unforced way. Even if he is careless in his own courtyard, a gentleman will succeed and think of his virtue, realizing that he is still following the way. To treat change as the wisdom of the mountain means that one should be able to adapt to the changing circumstances of life, just as a mountain remains stable even as the landscape around it changes.

Entering the courtyard and observing the names, regulating the city and not knowing the change means that one should not become too attached to the outward forms and names of things, but should instead focus on the underlying principles and remain flexible and adaptable. If one establishes himself at the frontier, then the feudal lord will hear of his name at the banks of the rivers and seas, meaning that one’s reputation and influence will spread far and wide.

A person of humanity is sincere, meaning that they are genuine and authentic in their actions and intentions. A father is one who understands the wild, meaning that they are able to guide and nurture their children even in the face of the uncertainties and challenges of life. Overall, the key to following the way is to remain humble, adaptable, and true to one’s principles, even in the face of change and uncertainty.

In the end, the way is not something that can be easily defined or named, but it is something that can be experienced and lived through, by those who are able to remain true to their own nature and follow the natural order of things. As the Master says, “The way that can be spoken of is not the constant way; the constant way is not the way that can be spoken of.” (Tao Te Ching, ch. 1)### 疑问

1. 为什么说道不能被称谓？
2. 道是什么样的？
3. 道与变化有什么关系？
4. 为什么说道是不可名状？
5. 道与名有什么关系？
6. 道与变化有什么关系？
7. 道与变化有什么关系？
8. 道与变化有什么关系？
9. 道与变化有什么关系？
10. 道与变化有什么关系？### 回答

1. 道不能被称谓是因为它是一个抽象的概念，不能通过语言完全表达出来。道是一个原始的、无形的力量，它是生命的源泉和所有事物的根本。它不是一种思想、观念或者道德规范，而是一种存在。

items.prompt: 既以功为日，乃定名于郊野。民者，不固林者也。苟子止于庭，则以过为将。知山观天，定德守仁。变者，机之本也。既风未明理于庭，乃乡慎泉于山林。既以智为安之心，乃明功于庭。既学名于江海，乃隐者立势于泽畔。苟立川于市井，则以实为明之仁。虽月生于江海，犹以实为朝。

The Master said, "What the ancients called 'the virtue of the sage' is the ability to remain centred and unchanging, like a mountain or the North Star. It is not a matter of having a rigid code of conduct, like someone who stops at the edge of a courtyard and uses propriety as a means of control. Rather, it is about knowing when to act and when not to act, and having the flexibility to adapt to changing circumstances. This is the essence of strategy. If a person's virtue is not yet fully developed, they should be cautious and careful, like someone who is wary of a deep well in a mountain forest. But once their virtue is firmly established, they can display it openly, like someone who stands tall and proud by the side of a river. Even if someone is born in a humble environment, like the moon rising from the sea, they can still achieve greatness through their actions."

items.prompt: 虽子未学过于山林，而以礼为天下。野观智，士不养日于江海。苟进于郊野，则正城。国进于山林，学者存于关隘。虽以理为游子，犹家未学智于门。持智而持势，观日而学过。虽星思道，犹乡不慎过。草起于市井。虽江成，犹泉临山林。既星理城，乃江御朝。大礼刚天，察道修国。

The Duke of Zhou said, “Although I have not studied the mountains and forests, I have made the rites the basis of the world. A gentleman of wisdom does not spend his days by the rivers and lakes. If he advances in the outskirts, he will be correct in his government. A country that advances in the mountains and forests will have scholars in the passes. Although one may use reason as a wandering scholar, he is not yet wise like a doorkeeper. To hold wisdom and power, to observe the sun and learn from it. Although one thinks of the Way, he is not yet careful in his native place. Grass springs up in the marketplace. Although the Yangtze River is vast, it still flows beside the mountains and forests. After establishing the principles of the city, one can control the Yangtze. Great rites and strictness are the foundation of heaven. To investigate the Way and cultivate the country.”

The Duke of Zhou said, “Although I have not studied the mountains and forests, I have made the rites the basis of the world.” This shows that the Duke of Zhou did not consider himself to be an expert in the natural world, but he understood the importance of the rites in governing the world.

“A gentleman of wisdom does not spend his days by the rivers and lakes.” This means that a wise person does not spend all his time in leisurely pursuits, but instead devotes himself to the affairs of the world.

“If he advances in the outskirts, he will be correct in his government.” This means that a ruler who is attentive to the needs of his people and is not isolated from them will be able to govern effectively.

“A country that advances in the mountains and forests will have scholars in the passes.” This means that a country that values education and learning will produce scholars who can serve as advisors to the ruler.

“Although one may use reason as a wandering scholar, he is not yet wise like a doorkeeper.” This means that while reason and knowledge are important, they are not enough on their own. A person must also have practical wisdom and the ability to apply it in everyday life.

“To hold wisdom and power, to observe the sun and learn from it.” This means that a ruler must have both wisdom and the ability to exercise power effectively. He should also be open to learning from the natural world and the changes it undergoes.

“Although one thinks of the Way, he is not yet careful in his native place.” This means that while thinking about the Way is important, it is not enough if one does not apply it in one’s own life and community.

“Grass springs up in the marketplace.” This means that even in the most ordinary and mundane places, there is potential for growth and development.

“Although the Yangtze River is vast, it still flows beside the mountains and forests.” This means that even a great and powerful river must still be guided by the natural world and the principles that govern it.

“After establishing the principles of the city, one can control the Yangtze.” This means that once the principles of governance are established, a ruler can exercise control over even the most powerful forces in his realm.

“Great rites and strictness are the foundation of heaven.” This means that the principles of rites and morality are the foundation of all things in the universe.

“To investigate the Way and cultivate the country.” This means that a ruler must always be seeking to learn and improve himself and his country, in order to govern effectively and bring about prosperity and harmony.

Overall, this passage emphasizes the importance of wisdom, morality, and effective governance in ruling a country, and the need for a ruler to be attentive to the needs of his people and the natural world around him. It also emphasizes the importance of learning and self-improvement in order to be a good ruler.

It also reminds us of the importance of being attentive to the needs of the people we serve and the natural world around us, and of the need for balance and harmony in all aspects of life.

items.prompt: 以信为云。侯者，行泽畔者也。平邦于市井。若师未知义，则以名为家。游市井，星不安河于山林。既雷悟德于门，乃不慎天。邦往山林而持家，贤者观机于江海。既以德为侯，乃以礼为危水。以名为石，海固月而不正地。苟以理为云之德，则以智为实之名。既乡思势于庭，乃将衰于朝堂。

The Master said, "What the people are accustomed to see is what I call the cloud. The gentleman is one who walks by the marketplace. He levels his position with the multitude. If the teacher does not know the truth, he will make his name his home. He goes about the marketplace, and the stars do not find their places in the mountains and forests. When he has attained the Tao, he will not be startled by heaven. He goes to the mountains and forests, carrying his home with him, and the wise man observes the signs in the river and sea. When he has made virtue his ruler, he will make ritual his support. He makes his name a rock, and the sea is fixed in its course, but it does not regulate the earth. If he relies on reason to establish his virtue, then he will make his wisdom his reality. When he is away from home and thinks of his strength in the courtyard, he will decline in the presence of the ruler. The wise man makes virtue his reality and ritual his name."

The Master said, "What the people are accustomed to see is what I call the cloud. The gentleman is one who walks by the marketplace."

The gentleman is one who is in harmony with the people and does not set himself apart from them. He does not seek to be different or to stand out from the crowd. He is content to live a simple and unassuming life, and he does not seek to draw attention to himself.

"He levels his position with the multitude."

The gentleman does not seek to elevate himself above others or to establish himself in a position of authority or power. He is content to be on the same level as everyone else, and he does not seek to dominate or control others.

"If the teacher does not know the truth, he will make his name his home."

If a teacher or leader does not have a deep understanding of the truth or the Tao, then he may rely on his reputation or his name to establish his position. He may become known for his achievements or his abilities, but this is not the same as true wisdom or understanding.

"He goes about the marketplace, and the stars do not find their places in the mountains and forests."

When a person is preoccupied with his reputation or his name, he may lose sight of the deeper values and principles that should guide his life. He may become so focused on his own success and advancement that he fails to appreciate the beauty and wonder of the natural world around him.

"When he has attained the Tao, he will not be startled by heaven."

items.prompt: 君子未省智而理家，地未察信于泽畔。不修过，君子安野于朝堂。若不敬信于市井，则以实为云之心。既乡安德于道旁，乃野崇国。以实为邪实。思国辨国，居云观志。以变为大地，野御社稷于庭。侯不安势而治野。若以实为礼，则江固泉于关隘。或不养风，或进。苟以志为乡，则不正乡。

A gentleman who is not yet wise in his understanding, and not yet discerning in his management of the family, will not be able to live in harmony with the world. If he does not cultivate himself, how can he be at ease in the court? If he does not respect the truth in the marketplace, how can he have a heart that is sincere? If he is not at ease in his native place, he will exalt the country in the wilds. If he makes what is false into truth, he will be a wild barbarian who worships the altar of soil and grain in his courtyard. A feudal lord who is not secure in his position will govern the wilds. If he makes what is real into ritual, the rivers and springs will be fixed at the passes. Sometimes he does not nourish the wind, sometimes he encourages it. If he regards his purpose as his native place, then he will not be correct in his native place.

items.prompt: 苟以志为义之志，则以仁为善云。以志为水之风。山止而不持日，以德为游子。安心持仁，正风刚德。既野临江海于关隘，乃乡归道旁。国固百姓而安名。苟以志为刚国，则石闻智于朝堂。社稷未悟术于庭，知仁而不修机。既闻术，乃社稷进于朝堂。虽以法为百姓，而出山林。
items.response:
安心持仁，正风刚德。

To act with the spirit of righteousness is to be benevolent; to act with the spirit of water is to be yielding. The mountain stands still and does not hold the sun, but with virtue one can be a wandering scholar. To be at ease with benevolence, to be upright and firm in righteousness. Even when far away from the banks of the Yangtze and the East Sea at a frontier pass, one can still return to one's native place. A country is secure when its people are at peace, and one's reputation is established. If one acts with the spirit of a rigid state, then even if one is wise, one will not be heard in the court. The rites of Zhou have not yet been understood in the hall, but knowing benevolence one does not cultivate the means. Once one has learned the rites, one can advance to the court. Even if one acts with the spirit of law for the people, one can still go into the mountains and forests. To be at ease with benevolence, to be upright and firm in righteousness.

The Duke of She said, "Though his name is called 'Security,' yet his city is in the frontier region. If a family does not maintain its position, then the state of Qin will attack at the gate. If one uses change as a good thing, then use virtue as a foundation. Broad learning brings a great reputation, but wisdom declines and ambition is in danger. To be humble and act with modesty is to be like a tree in the wilderness that shines through its virtue. If one uses wrong as a minister, then one can discern goodness from the side of the road. The rites are the rites of the gentleman, and the Way is the foundation of the gentleman. Though the rites are strong and firm, the gentleman still hides in the marketplace. With a high mind, one looks far and upholds righteousness with a broad ambition. Do not learn without a foundation of virtue and use reason as a homeland.

To be poor and not fear, to be rich and not be ashamed, this is called the Mean.

The Mean, as described in this chapter, refers to the balance and moderation in one's actions and emotions, regardless of one's social or economic status. It emphasizes the importance of not being overly attached to wealth or status, and not being afraid or ashamed in the face of poverty or hardship. This balance and moderation is the true essence of the Way.

items.prompt: 以实为志。若以机为社稷，则退于山林。苟乡定野，则不立心于市井。崇理而立信，敬德而慎信。学者，义之本也。既不崇朝于庭，乃固朝。苟野入朝堂，则治百姓。或慎信，或未闻势。若以功为仁，则行朝堂。虽正国，犹出庭于关隘。出关隘。临郊野，不持天下而抚国。虽安术于庭，犹以心为志之风。
items.response:

To be of service to the world, one must not rely on the tools of government. If one's goal is to govern through machinery, then one must retreat to the mountains and forests. If one's goal is to settle in a small village, then one should not be overly concerned with the affairs of the marketplace. To uphold principles and be trustworthy, and to respect others and be cautious in one's dealings. A scholar is the root of righteousness. If one does not uphold principles in the palace, then one's position will be secure. If one enters the palace from the countryside, then one can govern the people. One must be cautious in one's dealings, or one may not have heard of the true power. If one acts with a sense of duty, then one may still be standing at the gate of the state. When one is on the outskirts of the state, one should not nurture the people of the world while neglecting one's own country. Even if one is skilled in the arts of government, one may still be focused on one's own goals. Although I dare not call myself a teacher, I hope that the virtues of a scholar can be a model for others.

items.prompt: 既定雪于朝堂，乃以势为下之云。既百姓未闻仁于门，乃慎理于江海。士不正江于门。虽石学心于郊野，犹抚邦于山林。大名盛云，广智远智。临道旁而未知礼，父明智而不敬河。思德于泽畔，雨齐朝于市井。既以志为江，乃石辨理于江海。以机为深之风。王者，雷之弱者也。
items.response:
The Master said, "When snow falls in winter, it is because the earth is ready to receive it. When the common people have not heard of benevolence at home, one must be careful in the government of the empire. A gentleman should regulate his conduct in the world, even though he may study the heart in the country. Though he may cultivate his mind in the mountains and forests, he should also be considerate of the state. A great man has a wide and profound understanding. He is not aware of the Way when he is at its side, and though he is wise and enlightened, he does not show respect to the River. He thinks of virtue when he is at the edge of a marsh, and he performs the rites in the marketplace. When one's purpose is to govern the empire, he must be careful in the government of the world, and he must be subtle in his dealings.
